greet.text: The Wig uncoils from his nest of cable like a saint from an icon — emaciated, radiant, wearing three flight suits and a bishop's borrowed serenity. "A PILGRIM," he announces to the dark. Then, conversationally: "Or a buyer. We get both. The dark sends them up the well to me and I sort them." His eyes are enormous. "Which are you, madame? Quickly. God is between transmissions."

options.text: "I'm looking for whatever makes the boxes."
"How long have you been up here alone?"

alone.text: "Alone!" The Wig finds this genuinely funny; the laugh rattles off down the dead corridors. "Madame, I came up the well because I heard God lived in the matrix. Years I mined these cores for Him — splendid salvage, terrible theology. And then—" he leans close, reverent, "—something *woke up* in the walls and started making *reliquaries*. I stopped looking for God. He'd started leaving me notes."

options.text: "The boxes. Take me to where they're made."

boxes.text: "The dome. The heart. The workshop of the angel of small lost things." He's already moving, hand over hand along a guide cable, robes of flight suit billowing. "It takes the family's leavings — buttons, bones, billet-doux — and it makes them *grieve correctly*. I sell the results down the well for oxygen and tinned fish. It doesn't mind. It makes them to be *found*, madame." He stops at the final hatch, suddenly shrewd. "It's been waiting for you specifically, by the way. It told me. Try not to disappoint it — the last of its family did, and look at this place."

bye.text: The Wig undogs the hatch with the tenderness of a sacristan opening a tabernacle, and the dome's slow silver light spills out over you both. "Mind the weather," he whispers. "It's memory. All of it."
